coredata.dc:title: Wound therapy via a photo-responsively antibacterial nano-graphene quantum dots conjugate
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Na
Xu, Huanghuang
Sun, Shian
Guo, Pengyue
Wang, Yuan
Qian, Chuntong
Zhong, Yuanyuan
Yang, Dongzhi
coredata.dc:description: Common bacterial pathogens have become resistant to traditional antibiotics, representing an indispensable public health crisis. Photodynamic therapy (PDT), especially when common visible light sources are used as photodynamic power, is a promising bactericidal method. Based on the special photodynamic properties triggered by commonly available light emitting diode (LED) lamps, a kind of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) based composite system (termed GQDs@hMSN(EM)) was prepared through loading both GQDs and erythromycin (EM) into the hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticle (hMSN), aiming to achieve joint antimicrobial effect. Bacterial density experiments confirmed that GQDs@hMSN(EM) had combined antimicrobial effects from photodynamic effect and drug release on Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). In animal models, the healing degree of wounds infected by bacteria also confirmed that GQDs@hMSN(EM) group had the best therapeutic effect, with the significantly reduced inflammatory factors in blood. Different from traditional GQDs synthesized by solvothermal method, the as-prepared GQDs@hMSN can produce singlet oxygen (1O2) under light exposure to destroy the structure of bacteria, thus achieving highly efficient antimicrobial effect. The GQDs@hMSN(EM) in this work possesses good antimicrobial activity, sufficient drug loading, and controllable drug release ability, which provides a new opportunity for GQDs-based nanoplatform to enhance antimicrobial effect and reduce their drug resistance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Photodynamic therapy
Antibacterial

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In Vitro Antibacterial Activity of GQDs@hMSN Conjugates
Evaluation of Combined Antimicrobial Effect of GQDs@hMSN(EM) In Vitro
Staining of Living and Dead Bacteria

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Transmission electron microscopy images (A, B and C), dynamic laser scattering measurement (D) and optical properties (E) of GQDs@hMSN conjugates.
Antimicrobial effect (A) and dose-dependent behavior (B) of different GQDs and GQDs@hMSN.
The drug release of GQDs@hMSN(EM) (A) and antibacterial efficiency of EM, GQDs@hMSN and GQDs@hMSN(EM) with or without light (B).
A. Wound healing of test animals after the treatment with EM, GQDs, GQDs@hMSN and GQDs@hMSN(EM); B. Wound size treated by EM, GQDs, GQDs@hMSN and GQDs@hMSN(EM).
H&E staining and inflammatory factors treated by GQDs@hMSN conjugates.
Singlet oxygen yield of GQDs, GQDs/hMSN and RB.
Bacterial damage caused by GQDs and GQDs@hMSN.
A. DNA/RNA leak histogram from bacteria treated by GQDs and GQDs@hMSN; B. SEM images of bacteria treated by GQDs@hMSN.

head.ce:title: Wound therapy via a photo-responsively antibacterial nano-graphene quantum dots conjugate

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of New Drug Research and Clinical Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, Jiangsu 221004, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of New Drug Research and Clinical Pharmacy

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Common bacterial pathogens have become resistant to traditional antibiotics, representing an indispensable public health crisis. Photodynamic therapy (PDT), especially when common visible light sources are used as photodynamic power, is a promising bactericidal method. Based on the special photodynamic properties triggered by commonly available light emitting diode (LED) lamps, a kind of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) based composite system (termed GQDs@hMSN(EM)) was prepared through loading both GQDs and erythromycin (EM) into the hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticle (hMSN), aiming to achieve joint antimicrobial effect. Bacterial density experiments confirmed that GQDs@hMSN(EM) had combined antimicrobial effects from photodynamic effect and drug release on Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). In animal models, the healing degree of wounds infected by bacteria also confirmed that GQDs@hMSN(EM) group had the best therapeutic effect, with the significantly reduced inflammatory factors in blood. Different from traditional GQDs synthesized by solvothermal method, the as-prepared GQDs@hMSN can produce singlet oxygen (1O2) under light exposure to destroy the structure of bacteria, thus achieving highly efficient antimicrobial effect. The GQDs@hMSN(EM) in this work possesses good antimicrobial activity, sufficient drug loading, and controllable drug release ability, which provides a new opportunity for GQDs-based nanoplatform to enhance antimicrobial effect and reduce their drug resistance.

ce:section.ce:para: Skin is the largest organ of the human body which forms a protective barrier against harmful bacteria. Skin damage can usually result in bacteria penetration into the wound, leading to local infection, and even systemic sepsis [1]. Wound healing is a complex and dynamic process, including hemostasis, inflammation, proliferation and remodeling [2–4], where bacterial infection makes wound healing difficult. Microorganisms (especially E. coli and S. aureus) may compete with the host immune system to invade tissues, causing infection and severe tissue damage [5–8]. In the process of wound healing, uncontrollable bleeding and wound infection are the most significant factors to cause healing delay and even death [9–11]. Due to high morbidity and mortality, bacterial infections-related diseases have become one of the major public health problems of human beings.

Local administration of antibiotics can be used as the initial treatment for superficial wound infection [12]. However, the overuse of antibiotics and rapid activation of resistance genes in bacteria have resulted in strong resistance of pathogenic bacteria to traditional antibiotics [13–15], leading to millions of deaths worldwide. It is particularly urgent to develop a new class of drug that can reduce antibiotic resistance and have broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity and good biocompatibility.

Unlike antibiotics, photodynamic therapy (PDT) can impose non-specific attack on microorganisms by producing reactive oxygen species, especially singlet oxygen (1O2), when a PDT agent is irradiated by appropriate light (and oxygen is also present) [16]. Singlet oxygen can denature a series of enzymes inside the microorganisms (e.g. superoxide dismutase and catalase), which means that PDT will not lead to the production of resistant strains [17]. As a new photocatalyst, colloidal titanium dioxide has been widely used in antimicrobial applications and general disinfection. However, due to the broad band gap (3.2 eV) of titanium dioxide nanostructures, ultraviolet activation is needed for its antimicrobial activity. Therefore, it is necessary to modify titanium dioxide by doping with dyes or coupling with narrow band gap materials to extend its light excitation to the visible spectrum [18–21]. Simultaneously, researchers have begun to shift their attention to alternative semiconductors that can be stimulated by visible light and have antimicrobial activity [22–24]. Visible light activation is desired for photochemical antimicrobial agents, since they may inhibit the growth of pathogens under sunlight or incandescent light, thus promoting wound healing.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are one of derivatives from the graphene family [25–27]. They are composed of very small graphite fragments with a size less than 10 nm. GQDs with various properties can be prepared by different strategies. Most GQDs exhibited photothermal performance [28]. Recently, it is reported that GQDs has photodynamic characteristics rather than photothermal effect. It recently been shown to produce 1O2 [29], reflecting the potential of photodynamic applications, especially combined therapy [30,31]. The combination of antibiotics and photodynamic agents is a good choice for antibacterial treatment, which can overcome the resistance of antibiotics.

Silica materials have been recognized by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) as “generally safe” materials, which have good prospects in clinical application. Because of their non-toxicity, easy synthesis process, and low production cost, silica-based nanomaterials have been widely used in various biological fields [32–34]. Among various silica materials, hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles (hMSN) attracted great attention due to its controllable structure, large surface area, adjustable pore size, good thermal and chemical stability, excellent biocompatibility and easy surface modification [35]. Compared with MSN, hMSN can not only effectively accommodate drugs in the mesoporous channels, but also can maintain them in the hollow interior, greatly increasing the drug loading capacity.

In this study, a GQDs@hMSN(EM) composite antimicrobial system containing both GQDs and EM was constructed, which combined photodynamic antimicrobial material and traditional antibiotic. With a commonly available LED lamp as the light source, the synergistic antimicrobial activity of GQDs@hMSN(EM) was systematically investigated by comparing with that of traditional GQDs synthesized by solvothermal method. E. coli and S. aureus are used as representative Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria respectively to evaluate the antibacterial effect and mechanism. The therapeutic effect of GQDs@hMSN(EM) on bacteria infected wounds was investigated in a murine model.

The morphology and size of GQDs, hMSN and GQDs@hMSN were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and dynamic laser scattering (DLS) measurement. Calculated from Fig. 1A, 1B and 1C, the particle size of hMSN, GQDs@hMSN and GQDs was about 130 nm, 130 nm and 4.5 nm, respectively. DLS diagram in Fig. 1D showed that the particle size of the prepared GQDs and GQDs@hMSN conjugates was about 10 nm and 200 nm, respectively. Since the particle size of GQDs and GQDs@hMSN measured by DLS represented the hydrated particle size, it was slightly larger than that from TEM.

Erythromycin (EM) was capped into hMSN by the hydrophobic interaction to form GQDs@hMSN(EM) composite. As shown in Fig. 1E, EM exhibited a distinct characteristic absorption peak at 262 nm from the UV–Vis spectra. GQDs@hMSN(EM) also had significant absorption (indicated by the blue arrow) at the same wavelength, confirming that EM had been successfully loaded into GQDs@hMSN. At the same time, GQDs exhibited obvious absorption at 480 nm (the red arrow in Fig. 1E), and GQDs@hMSN and GQDs@hMSN(EM) showed the same characteristic absorption in this wavelength region, indicating that GQDs was successfully loaded in hMSN. The loading capacity of GQDs in hMSN was calculated to be 0.26 mg GQDs/mg hMSN. The stability of EM with the effect of singlet oxygen was also evaluated by high performance liquid chromatography of quadrupole time of flight-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-Q/TOF-MS) analysis. As shown in Fig. S1, no characteristic peak except for EM was detected, which verified the stability of EM and the possibility of combined photodynamic and antibiotic therapy.

By measuring the degree of rupture in red blood cells (RBC), the hemocompatibility of the prepared GQDs@hMSN conjugates was investigated. To quantitatively determine the hemolysis ratio, the RBCs dispersed in deionized water and phosphate buffer saline (PBS) were used as the positive and negative controls, respectively. Statistical analysis was performed on the hemolysis performance data from 7 different concentrations of GQDs and GQDs@hMSN. As shown in Fig. S2, the hemolysis rates of GQDs and GQDs@hMSN were less than 5% at each concentration, while it was only 3.8% and 4.2% at the highest concentration (80 μg/mL) used for GQDs and GQDs@hMSN, respectively. This result indicated that the material had good biocompatibility and could be used safely for biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In Vitro Antibacterial Activity of GQDs@hMSN Conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: By measuring the survival rate of E. coli and S. aureus, the antimicrobial activities of GQDs synthesized by traditional methods (traditional GQDs), newly prepared GQDs (GQDs) and hMSN-capped GQDs (GQDs@hMSN) were determined. Fig. S3 showed that compared with control group, the growth of E. coli and S. aureus did not change significantly in the traditional GQDs group. Data analysis showed that the survival rates of E. coli and S. aureus were 79.7 ± 1.7% and 72.2 ± 1.5% respectively at 4 μg/mL of traditional GQDs. In contrast, the GQDs prepared in our laboratory exhibited obvious antimicrobial effect at the same dosage compared with the control group. As shown in Fig. S3, after being treated by 4 μg/mL GQDs, the survival rate of E. coli and S. aureus respectively reduced to 45.7 ± 1.9% and 56.8 ± 2.0%, indicating a significant bacterial-killing effect. When the dose of GQDs increased to 8 μg/mL, the survival rates of E. coli and S. aureus decreased to 16.4 ± 2.1% and 23.6 ± 1.7%, respectively, indicating a dose-dependent antimicrobial activity for GQDs. After coating by hMSN, the survival rate of E. coli and S. aureus in the 8 μg/mL dose group was 没有全都提高 slightly increased to 14.7 ± 1.9% and 28.9 ± 2.1%, respectively, showing the antimicrobial activity of GQDs was not compromised significantly after loading into hMSN.

In order to verify that antimicrobial activity was from photodynamic effect, tradition-GQDs, the as-prepared GQDs and GQDs@hMSN with the same dose of 8 μg/mL based on the concentration of GQDs were incubated with E. coli and S. aureus with or without light exposure. As shown in Fig. 2A, with the irradiation of normal LED lamp, the antibacterial effect of the as-prepared GQDs on E. coli and S. aureus was significantly higher than that of tradition-GQDs. The results showed that the survival rate of E. coli only decreased from 67.3 ± 1.3% to 55.8 ± 1.9% (n ≥ 3) while the survival rate of S. aureus decreased from 76.4 ± 1.6% to 62.5 ± 2.1% (n ≥ 3) in the traditional GQDs group after light exposure. In contrast, GQDs synthesized in this work exhibited better regular light sensitivity. Under light exposure, the survival rate of E. coli and S. aureus decreased from 28.2 ± 2.2% to 8.6 ± 0.7% and 47.6 ± 1.0% to 12.7 ± 2.2% (n ≥ 3) respectively, indicating the existence of statistical significance between the two groups (P < 0.01). In GQDs@hMSN group, the survival rate of E. coli decreased from 31.9 ± 1.9% to 6.0 ± 0.6% (n ≥ 3) after light exposure, and the survival rate of S. aureus decreased from 40.8 ± 1.8% to 11.6 ± 1.9% (n ≥ 3). These results indicated that GQDs@hMSN and GQDs possessed similar antimicrobial activity, which was from photodynamic effect.

The dose dependence of GQDs@hMSN against E. coli and S. aureus with the assistance of light was tested and showed in Fig. 2B. The survival rates of E. coli decreased to 26.7 ± 3.0%, 22.4 ± 2.9%, 18.1 ± 1.8%, 8.75 ± 3.0% and 0.5 ± 3.5% (n ≥ 3) respectively after GQDs@hMSN treatment (at the dose of 4, 6.4, 8, 10.4 and 12 μg/mL, respectively). The gradually decreased survival rates confirmed that there was a clear correlation between GQDs@hMSN dose and its antibacterial activity. Similar to E. coli, the survival rates of S. aureus decreased to 25.4 ± 3.1%, 20.5 ± 2.7%, 15.9 ± 2.0%, 12.65 ± 3.0% and 0.64 ± 3.3% (n ≥ 3) respectively when treated with the same dose of GQDs@hMSN (4, 6.4, 8, 10.4 and 12 μg/mL, respectively).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of Combined Antimicrobial Effect of GQDs@hMSN(EM) In Vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The loading capacity of EM in GQDs@hMSN was calculated to be 812.5 mg/g by measuring the absorbance of EM in the supernatant and the total amount of EM. Drug release behavior in different pH media was also evaluated by measuring the absorbance of EM at 262 nm, trying to predict the release behavior of EM in vivo. As showed in Fig. 3A, the release of EM at the pH of 5.0 was significantly higher than that at the pH of 6.5 or 7.4. The drug release rate of EM was 59.2% in the medium of pH 5.0 at 48 h, while that in the medium of pH 6.5 and pH 7.4 was 43.0% and 19.8%, respectively. Due to the weak acidity of the wound in the infected environment [36], GQDs@hMSN(EM) had suitable drug-releasing ability for its loaded antibacterial drugs, and was thus useful for wound restoration.

In order to reduce bacterial resistance and enhance the antimicrobial effect, the synergistic antimicrobial effect of constructed GQDs@hMSN(EM) was studied. EM, GQDs@hMSN and GQDs@hMSN (EM) were treated with E. coli and S. aureus with or without light exposure. GQDs was maintained at the same dose in different treatment group. As showed in Fig. 3B, the survival rates of S. aureus in EM and GQDs@hMSN groups were 84.2 ± 2.1% and 15.9 ± 2.8% respectively, while those in GQDs@hMSN(EM) group decreased to 11.4 ± 1.9%. There were significant differences between GQDs@hMSN and GQDs@hMSN(EM) group. Similar to S. aureus treatment groups, the survival rates of E. coli treated with EM, GQDs@hMSN and GQDs@hMSN(EM) were 78.8 ± 1.8%, 18.1 ± 3.2% and 9.4 ± 2.8%, respectively. The antimicrobial efficacy of GQDs@hMSN(EM) was significantly better than that of GQDs@hMSN or EM alone.

Fig. S4 is the wound dressing after adsorbing saline, EM, GQDs, GQDs@hMSN and GQDs@hMSN (EM), respectively. The therapeutic effect of each group was evaluated by measuring the degree of wound healing. As shown in Fig. 4A, obvious infection was present in the wound of mice models before treatment. After 4 days of treatment, the wounds in control group and EM group showed severe redness and swelling. After 14 days of treatment, there was no significant change in the wound of control group, and the same condition appeared in EM group. In contrast, the wound healing of both GQDs group was obvious and the healing effect of GQDs-light group was better than that of GQDs-dark group, showing positive therapeutic effect due to the light exposure. Observing results of the two GQDs@hMSN groups, light assistance played a certain role in improving the wound healing although it was not obvious. Comparing the efficacy of GQDs group with that of GQDs@hMSN group, the antimicrobial effect of GQDs did not change significantly post the capping of hMSN. For the GQDs@hMSN(EM) group, the wound healing was significantly better than that of other groups on the 4th day of treatment, which was consistent with the experimental results in vitro, indicating obvious combined antimicrobial effect. The therapeutic effect of nanocomposites on wounds was evaluated by measuring the size of wounds. Results in Fig. 4B showed that the wound healing rates of control group, EM, GQDs-dark, GQDs-light, GQDs@hMSN-dark, GQDs@hMSN-light, GQDs@hMSN(EM)-dark and GQDs@hMSN(EM)-light group were 53%, 62%, 79.6%, 81.3%, 82.6%, 83.5%, 92.5% and 95.2%, respectively. All the groups including GQDs, GQDs@hMSN and GQDs@hMSN(EM) groups, not only had good therapeutic effect, but also exhibited good photosensitivity. Specifically, the therapeutic effect of GQDs@hMSN(EM) was significantly better than that of other groups.

In vivo bacterial infections often stimulate the immune system to produce white blood cells (WBCs), especially neutrophils, which can cause an antimicrobial response. On the 2nd and 14th day, the wound tissues were harvested and stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). In Fig. 5A, there was almost no complete epidermal structure after wound infection, and neutrophils (as shown by the black arrow) were still present after 2 days, and the granulation tissue of wound became thicker. After 14 days of treatment, there was no obvious epidermal structure in EM and control group, and there were still neutrophils in the dermal structure of control group. In the GQDs@hMSN(EM) group, the defect granulation tissue was replaced by collagen fibers, and almost complete recovery of epidermis could be observed, since the layered structure of epidermis and dermis could be seen (as shown by blue and red arrows).

The effects of nanosystems on inflammatory response were analyzed by detecting serum inflammatory index, including interleukin 6 (IL-6) and monocyte chemoattractant protein-1 (MCP-1). As shown in Fig. 5B, after 14 days of treatment, the levels of IL-6 and MCP-1 in GQDs@hMSN group were significantly lower than those in control group, suggesting the reduced inflammatory reaction from wound healing. With the material's killing effect on wound bacteria, the wound bacteria gradually decreased, and the wound began to heal. The experimental results showed that the combination of nano-drug-loading system and light therapy achieved effective and sustained antimicrobial properties.
A. H&E staining of the regenerated skin tissue after 2 and 14 days of treatment; B. Expression level of IL-6 and MCP-1 after EM, GQDs, GQDs@hMSN, GQDs@hMSN(EM) treatment.

The photodynamic bactericidal effect of the material was also confirmed by staining of live/dead bacteria. The red fluorescence of propidium iodide (PI) indicated the location of dead cells. Results in Fig. S5 and Table S1 confirmed that light exposure could obviously enhance the antimicrobial properties of GQDs@hMSN. To assess 1O2 generation ability of GQDs@hMSN conjugates, a chemical capture method in which Na2-ADPA was used as the capture agent and rose bengale (RB) as the standard photosensitizer (φ RB = 0.75 in water) was used. As shown in Fig. 6 , with light exposure, the absorbance of Na2-ADPA solution at 378 nm decreased gradually with the prolongation of irradiation time, indicating that the 1O2 produced by each experimental group increased gradually. From the variation of the profile, the ability of GQDs to produce singlet oxygen is slightly weaker than that of RB, while there was no significant difference between GQDs@hMSN and GQDs. The quantum yield of the GQDs and GQDs@hMSN was calculated [37] to be 0.48 and 0.42, respectively. In order to verify the ability of GQDs and GQDs@hMSN to produce ROS under light conditions, cell experiments were also performed using DCFH-DA probes as receptors for reactive oxygen species (ROS). Fig. S6 showed that the cytoplasm treated with GQDs and GQDs@hMSN under light conditions exhibited significant green fluorescence, demonstrating that ROS can be produced under GQDs and GQDs@hMSN treatment.

Damage to cell membranes can lead to the leakage of DNA and RNA from cytoplasm, leading to serious dysfunction of bacteria and ultimately bacterial death. In this work, the integrity of bacterial cell membrane before and after GQDs and GQDs@hMSN treatment was studied by measuring the characteristic absorption of DNA and RNA at 260 nm, and the antimicrobial mechanism of GQDs@hMSN was verified. As showed in Fig. 7A, compared with control group, GQDs and GQDs@hMSN broke the cell membrane of E. coli and S. aureus under non-illumination conditions. The DNA/RNA leakage of bacteria in GQDs and GQDs@hMSN treatments increased significantly under illumination, suggesting that the damage of bacteria was enhanced by visible light, which may be caused by photocatalysis of GQDs to produce 1O2 to release intracellular DNA and RNA. The membrane integrity of E. coli and S. aureus was monitored after being treated by GQDs@hMSN conjugates under light and dark conditions. As shown in Fig. 7B, both the E. coli and S. aureus in control group showed a smooth and intact membrane structure. In contrast, the membrane of E. coli and S. aureus was shrieked and ruptured after being treated by GQDs@hMSN conjugates, which led to the leakage of DNA, RNA, or proteins in bacteria, and eventually caused serious bacterial damage. This was mainly due to the destruction of cell structure and the death of bacteria was caused by 1O2 produced from GQDs-containing composites.

The GQDs@hMSN(EM) nano-antimicrobial system constructed in this work maintained the photodynamic properties of GQDs and the bactericidal efficacy of EM. The GQDs prepared in this study had better antimicrobial properties than the traditional GQDs. The composite system has obvious combined antimicrobial properties for both Gramsci-positive and Gramsci-negative bacteria. The degree of wound recovery, blood inflammatory factors and biopsy all confirmed that GQDs@hMSN(EM) had obvious therapeutic advantages over drug alone. The study on the antimicrobial mechanism of nanomaterials proved that photodynamic effect played a crucial role in the treatment process. It could destroy the structure of bacteria by releasing 1O2, thus achieving high efficiency of antimicrobial effect. The constructed nanosystem combined the special properties of nanomaterials, photodynamic effects and drugs, solve the limitations of relatively independent treatment and single use of antibiotics in clinic.

E. coli and S. aureus were purchased from Beijing Zhongke Quality Inspection Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Agar power was acquired from Vicmed. Yeast extract and tryptone were acquired from Oxoid. Live/dead bacterial fluorescent staining kit and DAPI were got from Nanjing KeyGen Biotech Co., Ltd. 2′, 7′-dichlorofluorescent yellow diacetate (DCFH-DA) was purchased from Sigma. The disodium salt of 9,10-anthracene dipropionic acid (ADPA) was acquired from Abcam. N, N-dimethyldodecylamine, thiophene boric acid, bromobenzyl bromide were purchased from Alfa Aesar. Potassium persulfate, phosphorus pentoxide, hydrogen peroxide, 2% glutaraldehyde, 4-(triphenylphosphine) palladium, 4% polyformaldehyde, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) (99.99%), triethanolamine (TEA) (98%) and erythromycin were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. Cetyltrimethylammonium chloride (CTAC) (25%) was purchased from J&K Scientific. Graphite powder, column chromatography silica gel and other organic chemical reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. and directly used without further purifications.

Tradition-GQDs were synthesized by the method reported in literature [38]. GQDs@hMSN with uniform particle size was synthesized using a modified sodium carbonate etching process. GQDs were prepared by our previous method [39]. In brief, 4-bromobenzyl bromide (1 mmol) and N, N-dimethyldodecylamine (1.3 mmol) were dissolved in 20 mL dichloromethane/methanol (V:V = 3:2) solution and reacted at room temperature (RT) for 12 h in N2 atmosphere. The reaction solution was washed and precipitated by anhydrous ether to obtain the reaction product (named compound 1). Compounds 1 (0.86 mmol), 3-thiophenoboric acid (1 mmol), sodium carbonate (4.7 mmol) and 4-(triphenylphosphine) palladium (0.17 mmol) were dissolved in 30 mL ethanol/deionized water (V:V = 2:1) and reacted at 90 °C for 6 h in N2 atmosphere. The product was extracted by dichloromethane and purified by silica gel column and thin layer chromatography to form a crystalline compound (called compound 2). The solution of compound 2 was dropped into FeCl3 solution and reacted at RT for 48 h to get the dark red polymer solid (called PT2). It was dissolved in deionized water and its pH was adjusted to 10.0 by NaOH. The GQDs@hMSN was prepared by the StÖber method [40]. The GQDs aqueous solution was obtained by heating the solution in a high-pressure reactor at 160 °C for 24 h. A certain amount of TEOS was added to the aqueous solution of GQDs, and dSiO2/GQDs was obtained by reaction at RT for 40 min. The reaction solution was centrifuged and washed with 17% ethanol for three times, and then re-dispersed in deionized water. The prepared dSiO2/GQDs solution was added to CTAC solution to adjust the solution to alkalinity and allowed to stir for 1.5 h at RT. 135 μL TEOS was dropped to it at a rate of 27 μL/min and continued to react for 1 h at 80 °C. GQDs@hMSN was obtained through etching by sodium carbonate for 40 min at 50 °C. The product was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min, washed by double distilled water and redispersed in anhydrous ethanol for further use. By measuring the fluorescence intensity, the excessive GQDs can be determined. According to the amount of added GQDs, the loading capacity of GQDs in hMSN was calculated.

GQDs@hMSN(EM) was obtained by adding 1 mg/mL EM to GQDs@hMSN solution and reacting at 37 °C for 24 h. The product was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, washed with absolute ethanol for 3 times, and then redispersed in saline for further use.

The encapsulation and release properties of EM in GQDs@hMSN were determined by sulfuric acid derivatization. Briefly, 2 mL sample was mixed with 5 mL 5 mol/L H2SO4 and heated in boiling water for 3 min. After diluted by ethanol and allowed to derive for 20 min, the characteristic absorption strength of the solution at 262 nm was determined. Drug loading was calculated by comparing the total EM with unloaded EM. The drug release behavior of GQDs@hMSN(EM) at 37 °C was evaluated by determining the absorbance of derived EM after culturing with different media with pH 5.0, 6.5 and 7.4 for 2, 4, 8, 16, 24, 36, 48 and 72 h, respectively. To ensure the efficacy of erythromycin, the possible oxidation product by singlet oxygen was detected by HPLC-Q/TOF-MS analysis according to the oxidation reactions and detection methods list in previous reports [41,42].

The blood compatibility of GQDs and GQDs@hMSN was investigated by measuring the characteristic absorption of heme at 398 nm, which was caused by cell rupture. The samples with concentrations of 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70 and 80 μg/mL was evaluated using deionized water and saline as positive and negative control respectively. 1 mL sample was incubated with 100 mL blood at 37 °C, and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min to remove the whole erythrocyte and erythrocyte fragments. The supernatant obtained by centrifugation was precipitated in a mixture of ethanol and hydrochloric acid (39:1). The supernatant was centrifuged and measured at 398 nm. The percentage of hemolysis was calculated using the following equation: Hemolysis (%) = (sample absorbance - negative control absorbance)/(positive control absorbance - negative control absorbance) × 100%.

E. coli and S. aureus were cultured in LB liquid medium under normal conditions (37 °C, 200 rpm). The antimicrobial effects of different samples on E. coli and S. aureus were investigated by plate colony counting. Normal saline was used as control group, and four parallel control groups were set up in each group. After culturing a certain concentration of the sample with bacteria for 12 h, the number of bacterial colonies was determined. The survival rate of bacteria was calculated by comparing the number of colonies in the experimental group with that in the control group. For the groups with light exposure, the bacteria were firstly treated by ordinary LED lamp (5 W; distance: 40 cm) for 15 min before incubating in incubator.

Mice about 6-week old (half male and half female) were fed for one week before the experiment to adapt to the environment. Under sterile condition, the mice were shaved, and two symmetrical wounds of 1 × 1 cm2 were cut on the back, deep to the fascia. E. coli and S. aureus were dripped on the wound site, and sealed with a medical film to allow infection. The infected mice were randomly divided into 8 groups: control group, EM group, GQDs-dark group, GQDs-light group, GQDs@hMSN-dark group, GQDs@hMSN-light group, GQDs@hMSN(EM)-dark group and GQDs@hMSN(EM)-light group. The sterile dressing [43] made by Beijing University of Chemical Technology was used to adsorb therapeutic drugs, while the control group was sterile saline. The equilibrium swelling rate of aseptic dressings was 22.4%. Drug-loaded dressings were applied to the wounds of infected mice, and the wounds were sealed properly with indwelling needles and film, in order to isolate the wounds from the outside for avoiding multiple infections caused by mixed bacteria. The light exposure group was irradiated for 15 min. The wound dressing was changed every two days, and the healing of the wound was observed and measured. The healing rate was calculated at 0, 4, 7 and 14 days after treatment. Interleukin-6 (IL-6) and monocyte chemoattractant protein (MCP-1) were detected by ELISA to evaluate the effect of GQDs@hMSN conjugates on inflammatory response. The wound site and adjacent normal skin (day 2 and 14) were collected and H&E analysis was performed to evaluate the therapeutic effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Staining of Living and Dead Bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: The antimicrobial activity of nanomaterials was investigated by fluorescence staining kit of living/dead bacteria and confocal laser microscopy. For each kind of bacteria, the experiment was divided three groups: control group, GQDs@hMSN-black group and GQDs@hMSN-light group. E. coli and S. aureus were respectively incubated with each group in incubator for 4 h, followed by 3 μL fluorescent dye (SYTO 9:PI = 1:1) adding and allow to incubate for 15 min to stain. The bacterial suspension was centrifuged and observed under laser confocal microscopy, where the green fluorescence (SYTO 9, Ex/Em = 539/570–620 nm) denoted living bacteria, and the red fluorescence (PI, Ex/Em = 470/490–540 nm) denoted dead bacteria.

The ROS-sensitive probe, DCFH-DA, was selected to detect intracellular ROS production. Once DCFH-DA enters the cell, it can be converted to DCFH (a non-fluorescent polar derivative) by intracellular esterase. DCFH can then be rapidly oxidized by ROS to form a strong green fluorescent 2′,7′-Dichlorofluorescein (DCF). The 4 T1 cells were incubated with GQDs and GQDs@hMSN at a certain concentration for 3 h and then incubated with 10 μmol/mL DCFH-DA for 20 min. After being washed twice by PBS and irradiated by LED lamp for 15 min, the cells were stained with DAPI and observed under confocal laser microscopy (Ex/Em = 490/525 nm).

1O2 quantum yield measurements was carried out by a chemical method, where water-soluble Na2-ADPA was used as the [1]O2-trapping agent, and RB was used as the standard photosensitizer. 60 mL Na2-ADPA solution (1 mg/mL) was added to 1.5 mL sample solution, and the absorbance of Na2-ADPA at 378 nm was recorded at different time to obtain the attenuation rate of photosensitization process. The 1O2 quantum yield was calculated according to the following equation:
φ GQDs
=
φ RB
×
K GQDs
×
A RB
/
K RB
×
A GQDs
where, K GQDs and K RB are the decomposition rate constants of Na2-ADPA by the GQDs and RB, respectively. A GQDs and A RB represent the absorbance of GQDs conjugates and RB, respectively, which are determined by integration of the optical absorption bands in the wavelength range 400–700 nm. φ RB is the 1O2 quantum yield of RB, and which is 0.75 in water.

The morphological changes of bacteria due to GQDs@hMSN conjugates effect were measured by SEM under different conditions. A certain concentration of bacterial suspension was added to GQDs@hMSN and incubated in an incubator at 37 °C for 1 h. After incubation, bacterial samples were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 5 min and washed twice by PBS. The collected bacterial sediments were fixed at 4 °C with 2% glutaraldehyde. After that, the bacterial samples were treated according to the routine SEM biological sample preparation process, and the morphological changes of bacteria were observed with SEM.

SPSS 20.0 statistical software was used to analyze the experimental data. The data between the two groups were compared by t-test and one-way ANOVA test. P < 0.05 showed that there was statistically difference between the two groups. P < 0.01 indicate there was significant difference between the two groups.

Na Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - Original Draft, Writing - Review & Editing, Huanghuang Xu: Methodology, Writing - Review & Editing, Shian Sun: Data curation, Pengyue Guo: Resources, Data curation, Yuan Wang: Investigation, Chuntong Qian: Methodology, Yuanyuan Zhong: Validation, Dongzhi Yang: Supervision

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province, China (BK20161173), and Xuzhou Natural Science Foundation, China (KC18201 and KC18108). We are grateful to Professor Ma Guiping of Beijing University of Chemical Technology for the synthesis of the drug carrier.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotobiol.2020.111978.
